verdict.reasoning:
Tournament scores:
  a: 7.0 points
  c: 5.5 points
  b: 1.0 points

comparisons.reasoning: Implementation A keeps core simulation logic (simulation.js, model.js, variable.js) completely generic: runSimulation only calls model.evaluate(); Model accepts any evaluator function; Variable accepts any sampler. Adding a new model requires only a new evaluator module and UI wiring in app.js—no changes to simulation, model, or variable. New distributions are additive (new function in distributions.js) and Variable.distribution(sampler) already supports any sampler. Implementation B's Python side has no single core simulation—each model implements its own loop in calculate()—and main.py uses if/else plus direct imports for model selection, so new models require editing main.py. NPVModel hardcodes np.random.normal; there is no generic variable/distribution abstraction, so new variable types or distributions require modifying existing model code. Thus A has clear extension points (evaluators, distribution functions, DISTRIBUTIONS registry) and favors adding new code over modifying existing core logic; B requires editing main.py and model internals for both new models and new distributions.

The task explicitly requires that 'the user should be able to choose the model' and that for the NPV model the result be visualized as an area chart (cone of uncertainty) with 50th percentile and +/- 1 and 2 sigma in one chart. Implementation A (variation-b) implements the NPV logic and cone visualization correctly in Python (models/npv_model.py, utils/visualization.py, main.py) with proper inputs (cashflows, discount rate, initial investment), timeline output, and cone that widens over time. However, the web app (index.html, app.js) is a separate Monte Carlo variable simulator with no model selector and no NPV or cone chart—so the user of the delivered app cannot choose the model or see the NPV cone. Model 'choice' in A exists only as a hardcoded variable in main.py, not as a user-facing feature. Implementation B (variation-c) delivers a single coherent web app: a model dropdown lets the user choose between 'Summation (default)' and 'Net Present Value (NPV)'; when NPV is selected, the UI shows NPV configuration (initial investment, discount rate, yearly cashflows), runs the timeline simulation, and displays an area chart with 2-sigma and 1-sigma bands, 50th percentile line, and break-even (NPV=0) line, with a legend—all in one chart. The cone widens over time because uncertainty accumulates across years. Thus B satisfies every explicit requirement (NPV model inputs, timeline, break-even visibility, user model choice, area chart, cone of uncertainty, 50th and +/- 1 and 2 sigma together) in one user-facing application, while A leaves the core requirement of user model choice and the NPV cone visualization outside the actual user interface.

Both implementations satisfy the core requirements: an NPV model with cashflows, discount rate, and initial investment; a timeline of cumulative value over years; user-selectable model; and an area chart showing the cone of uncertainty with 50th percentile and ±1σ and ±2σ bands. Implementation B is more faithful to the task for these reasons: (1) Cashflows: The task says the model 'takes cashflows' (plural). B uses an explicit list of yearly cashflows (add year, each with its own distribution), matching multiple cashflow inputs. A uses a single annual cashflow variable sampled each year, which is simpler but does not match the plural 'cashflows' or support different assumptions per year. (2) Break-even: The task requires that 'the break-even point can be visually identified.' B draws an explicit NPV=0 reference line (dashed) on the chart, so break-even is directly visible where the band crosses zero. A relies on the user inferring the crossing and does not draw a zero line. (3) Sigma bands: For a 'cone of uncertainty,' percentile-based ±1σ and ±2σ (e.g. 15.87th/84.13th and 2.28th/97.72th) are the usual interpretation and remain meaningful for non-normal distributions. B uses these percentiles; A uses mean ± k×stdDev, which is parametric and less robust for skewed or multi-modal outcomes. (4) Intent: The description emphasizes flexible inputs (cashflows, discount rates, initial investment) and clear visual identification of break-even. B's per-year cashflow configuration and explicit zero line better match that intent. A is a valid, simpler implementation with a clean cone chart and safe edge-case handling (e.g. scaleX when length ≤ 1), but B better fulfills both the letter and the purpose of the task.

A keeps the feature in one codebase and one entry point: a single JS app that does both the sum model and the NPV model with cone chart. Its abstractions (Variable, Model with evaluator function, npvEvaluator, getTimelineSummary, renderConeChart) are all used directly for that feature; there is no abstract base class and no second runtime. B splits the feature across two stacks: a Python script (main.py + NPVModel + matplotlib) with hardcoded inputs and no UI, and a JS app that only does the sum model (no NPV, no cone). B introduces a FinancialModel ABC that has only one implementation (NPVModel), so the ABC exists only to support that single class. get_timeline() duplicates the return value of calculate(), adding a redundant API. So B has more incidental complexity: an abstraction that only backs one concrete class, a redundant method, and two separate codebases for what A does in one place. A has more lines and a custom SVG cone (cone-chart.js) but no extra abstraction layers or supporting-only constructs; the core flow (evaluator → simulation → statistics → display) stays easy to follow in one language and one app.

Implementation A is more faithful to the task. (1) Explicit requirement 'The user should be able to choose the model': A provides a model dropdown (Sum vs NPV) in the UI and shows NPV-specific inputs and the cone chart when NPV is selected. B has no model choice in the user-facing app: the web app (index.html + app.js) only supports the Sum model with variables; the NPV logic and cone visualization exist only in a separate Python script (main.py) with hardcoded inputs and no UI for model selection or parameters. (2) Task asks for cashflows, discount rates, and initial investment: A accepts configurable variables for both discount rate and cashflows (with distributions), plus initial investment and years in the UI. B's Python NPV model takes a single fixed discount rate and per-year cashflow params, and there is no UI for the user to set these. (3) Result as a timeline and cone visualization: Both implement a timeline of cumulative NPV and an area chart with 50th percentile and ±1σ and ±2σ bands in one chart; A renders it in the browser as part of the same app, B only via the script with matplotlib. (4) Intent: The spec implies one application where the user selects the model and sees the NPV cone. A delivers that; B splits functionality so the cone is not available where the user would 'choose' anything. A therefore satisfies all explicit requirements and the intended single-app, user-driven workflow; B implements NPV and the cone correctly in isolation but omits user model choice and an integrated UI for NPV.

Implementation A (variation-b) has less incidental complexity. (1) Lines: A implements the NPV cone in ~166 lines of Python across 4 files (main.py, base.py, npv_model.py, visualization.py); B uses ~250+ lines in npv-model.js and chart.js plus ~165 lines of NPV-specific code in app.js (~415+ total). (2) Abstractions: A introduces an abstract base class (FinancialModel) that is implemented only by NPVModel, so that ABC is the main extra construct. B introduces no ABC but adds MODELS, updateModelUI, buildNPVUI, addNPVCashflowRow, renumberCashflows, collectNPVConfig, runSelectedModel, displayChart, and extends buildVariableRow with setDefaults/labelText—more surface area. (3) Supporting-only constructs: In A, base.py exists only to define the interface for the single concrete model. In B, renumberCashflows exists only for the NPV cashflow list; setDefaults and labelText exist to support NPV (and default) rows; updateModelUI and runSelectedModel exist only to support the model selector and branching—several constructs exist purely to support others. (4) Cognitive load: A’s flow is linear (main → NPVModel.calculate() → plot_npv_cone) in one stack; B requires following model selection, NPV UI build, config collection, simulation, stats, and chart rendering across branching app logic and manual SVG. Overall, A is shorter, has one clear incidental abstraction (the ABC), and a simpler pipeline; B achieves in-browser NPV in one language but with more code and more supporting glue.

Implementation A keeps a single, generic core: one runSimulation(iterations, model) that only calls model.evaluate(), and one Model class that accepts any evaluator function(variables). Adding a new model requires no changes to simulation.js or model.js—only a new evaluator (e.g. in a new file) and UI wiring in app.js. Implementation B duplicates the simulation loop: runSimulation in simulation.js and runTimelineSimulation in npv-model.js do the same thing (loop and call model.evaluate()). That invites more runXYZSimulation variants for new result shapes and blurs what counts as core. B also uses two model shapes (Model with variables vs NPVModel with config), so extension is less uniform. For new variable types and distributions, both require the same edits (distributions.js, DISTRIBUTIONS registry, and createVariableFromUI switch in app.js); neither has a plugin-style extension point there. A wins on clear extension points for models, no duplication of core simulation logic, and a single contract (evaluator function) that keeps the design resilient to new model requirements.

B is easier to extend for the given criteria. (1) Core simulation logic: In both, the simulation loop (run N iterations, call model.evaluate(), collect results) stays unchanged when adding models. In B that lives in simulation.js and in runTimelineSimulation in npv-model.js; new models only add new model classes and possibly new runner helpers, without editing existing loop code. (2) Variable types and distributions: In B, every model (summation and NPV) depends only on Variable.sample(); NPVModel takes Variables for initial investment, discount rate, and each cashflow and never assumes a distribution. So new variable types or distributions are added only in distributions.js and in the UI (DISTRIBUTIONS + one case in createVariableFromUI)—no changes to any model or simulation logic. In A, the JS side matches that pattern, but A also has a Python NPV model that hardcodes np.random.normal(params['mean'], params['std']) for cashflows. Adding a new distribution there would require changing npv_model.py and thus model/simulation code. (3) Adding new models: In B, MODELS and the app’s runSelectedModel/updateModelUI require edits for each new model (orchestration only); the simulation runners and Variable/distribution layers are untouched. In A, the JS app has no model selector (summation only), and the Python main.py uses an if/else on selected_model, so new models force edits to main.py and, for NPV-like models, to model code that is not distribution-agnostic. (4) Resilience: B keeps a single contract (evaluate()) and models that are fully distribution-agnostic, so new requirements (e.g. new distributions or variable types) are handled by adding code in one place (distributions + UI) rather than modifying core or model logic. A’s mixed JS/Python design and distribution-specific Python NPV make it less resilient. Therefore B wins.

A has less incidental complexity. (1) Lines and abstractions: A uses fewer lines (e.g. app.js 207 vs 307, npv-model 27 vs 90, cone-chart 120 vs chart 161) and fewer concepts—a single evaluator function npvEvaluator(initialInvestment, years) and one runSimulation path vs B’s NPVModel class, runTimelineSimulation, and getTimelineStatistics. (2) Cognitive load: A keeps one mental model—evaluator, runSimulation, then getSummary or getTimelineSummary—with timeline stats as the same mean/σ-per-time-point idea as getSummary. B introduces two parallel flows (Model+runSimulation+getSummary vs NPVModel+runTimelineSimulation+getTimelineStatistics) and extra UI state (npvConfig, npvCashflowRows, renumbering). (3) Supporting-only constructs: In B, setDefaults exists mainly for NPV presets; runTimelineSimulation only for NPVModel; getTimelineStatistics only for timelines; and buildNPVUI, renumberCashflows, collectNPVConfig, addNPVCashflowRow, and npvCashflowRows exist to support the per-year NPV UI. A reuses buildVariableRow and addVariableRow for both modes via a single parameterized list/rowsMap and has no NPV-only class or timeline-only runner, so no comparable support-only scaffolding.
